None of this was in the handover document, and none of it could have been. It only comes from asking early.
On Monday she takes the reins of the Canadian division, and the first decision is to make no big decision for ninety days.
Everyone expects a senior hire to hit the ground running, but the smartest ones slow down first to speed up later.
Size up the culture before you try to move it, or you will spend your mandate correcting a problem you invented.
Lean on Gordon. He made the fast mistakes so you do not have to, and his errors are now your shortcuts.
Gordon admitted that he had been wrong about the restructure.
A coordinator explained that the reorganizations had all been reversed.
He warned me not to mistake their politeness for agreement.
Ruth recommended waiting, and urged me to build a simple entry plan.
One week into your listening tour. Before you tell me your plan, tell me what you actually heard.

A coordinator explained that the last three plans had all been reversed, so the team waits out any new one. And a teacher admitted that morale was low, but insisted that the cause was never being asked, not the workload.
Good, that is more than I heard in a year. And the regional director? He is the one people manage carefully.
He warned me not to mistake their politeness for agreement, and pointed out that a Canadian team will rarely say no to my face. So I am reading the room from their vantage point, not mine.
So people will push you to act. What do you tell them when they ask why you have not fixed anything yet?

That I am still listening, on purpose. Ruth urged me to treat the ramp-up period as learning, not delay, and you warned me not to spend the mandate before I understand the question. So I am earning the credibility first, and I am leaning on you to get there faster.
Gordon told me he had restructured the team too fast, and admitted that he had been wrong about almost all of it.
A coordinator explained that past reorganizations had all been reversed, so the team waits out new plans.
The regional director warned me not to mistake their politeness for agreement.
Ruth urged me to treat the ramp-up period as learning, not delay, and to build a simple entry plan.
Credibility is not spent with the title; it is earned in the first quarter, one honest conversation at a time.

Danielle, it is Gordon, recording this before I hand you the keys on Monday. I ran this division for six years, so let me give you the one thing the handover document will not. When I arrived, I was so sure of what needed fixing that I restructured the academic team in my first month, and I was wrong about almost all of it, because I had changed the answer before I understood the question. So here is what I would do differently if I were you. Do not decide anything for ninety days. Spend that time on a listening tour instead. Sit with the frontline staff, the people who run the classrooms, not just the directors, and ask them what is actually broken and what you must not touch. They told me things in year three that would have saved me year one, and the only reason they told me late is that I did not ask early. You have a real mandate here, and the temptation will be to spend it fast to prove you deserve it. Resist that. Credibility in this office is not given with the title. It is earned in the first quarter, one honest conversation at a time, and once you have it, everything else gets easier.

Danielle, it is Ruth, your coach. You asked me how to handle the first ninety days without either freezing or overreaching, so here is the short version. The ramp-up period is not wasted time, even though it will feel like it, because you are used to operating at full speed and suddenly you cannot. That discomfort is the learning curve, not a sign that you are failing. The mistake almost every senior hire makes is to treat the transition period as an audition, so they change something visible in week two just to look decisive. Do the opposite. Build a simple entry plan with three phases, learn, decide, act, and do not let anyone push you out of phase one early. When a stakeholder challenges you and asks why you have not fixed anything yet, that is not a threat, it is your cue to say, plainly, that you are still listening on purpose. And lean on Gordon. He made the fast mistakes so you do not have to, and a predecessor who is honest about what went wrong is the most useful person you have in the first quarter. Size up the culture before you try to move it.
